speaker: interviewer
text: We're good. Hang on. OK, we're back on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So the irony of it all was she didn't want to leave her money. In 1935, the decree in Germany was that everybody-- every Jewish person-- who had a savings account, it was closed. They couldn't take any money out. It was called a [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So all her money she brought into the marriage-- because it was her money, according to the conventional contract they signed-- that he would never touch-- my father would never touch her money. Something like $150,000 was in the bank from her family. She was apparently well-to-do from her family. And when she's talking to my dad, she says, I can't leave my money. What do you mean, you can't leave your money? It's not your money. It already belongs to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess my mother was overweight. She was about five foot tall. Weighed about 150. My father was overweight. Was about five foot three with 170 on him. She was afraid to go. Just fear. Which is not that unnatural, and it's not that unusual. So my dad said, we're leaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left. Again, no questions asked. We go to the train station. And this-- he bought some tickets. We enter. We enter a wagon, and it has different compartments. We go into a compartment and sit on the windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm about 12 and a half at this stage of the game. And from now on, I begin to wake up. Suddenly there is a veil of the ice. I begin to see-- questioning what is going on, but I begin to see what is happening. While we're waiting for the train to move out of the station, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the group of eight men, accompanied by two fellows-- one in the rear, one in front-- come toward our wagon. The two fellows, the back and the front, they were Gestapo people. Gestapo people-- everybody knew them. They were-- they had felt hats, leather coats, certain suits, certain attitudes. It's almost like the policeman in America. Sears and Roebuck suit, and he's got a gun in a bag. You know exactly who he is. Exactly the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They come into the wagon. Knock on the door of our compartment. My father opens the door. Says, is there room here? Yes. So these people sit in our compartment, two on each side. I face my dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the peculiar thing is that they kept their hats on. That's very impolite in Germany. In Germany, when you greet somebody, you take your hat off. When you go into a restaurant, you don't keep your hat on. You take it off. When you go any place, you take your hat off. That's etiquette. And I don't quite understand this whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the train moves out. And about 20, 30 minutes into the ride, one of the gentlemen looks at my dad and says, are you going to Cologne? And my father says, yes. Five minutes later-- excuse me, again. Are you, by any chance, Jewish? And my father says, yes. And then they look at me. There's the little non-looking Jew there. And my father says, that's my son. Perfectly safe. So they take all their hats off, and they had no hair. Now, I know what a bald head was like. My father had-- head was half bald. But they had shaven heads. What's a shaven head? So my dad says-- looked-- he just looked at him. And he says, he were just released from Buchenwald. Those were some of the German Jews who got arrested-- and the Polish Jews-- in the pogrom night. And they were sent to Buchenwald, to Dachau, to Sachsenhausen. And they were released. And they went back to Cologne. So my father looks at him and says, so what happened? And he looks at him. He says, we can't talk. The Gestapo warned us never to say anything what happened there, because they have long arms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Permeating to this now, when I went-- continued to go with my dad, and I go ahead of time, he educated me. What made Germany Germany. I learned through him, in the coming years, what made Germany Germany. Fear. Fear of fear itself. This is what it was all about, as you will see later on in the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrive in Cologne at 6 o'clock in the morning. There are thousands of people, from Austria, from Germany, Jews, communists. They all want to leave the country, and they can't, for some inexplicable reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father registered into a hotel. Sort of a-- a-- a 'pension hotel,' they call it. Like bed and breakfast. He apparently had that all prearranged already. Because a lady greeted him very friendly, and so on. We stayed eight days in Cologne. And he was showing me the city. I had a good time with him. Listen, I was alone with him. For once, I got to know my dad. And he told me all kinds of stories from the past, from his life, from the family life, from Berlin, Germany, per se. All I can remember is that once a month, we had to go to my aunt. And there was a-- a-- a concert. There were two violins, two pianos. And the little fellow fell asleep most of the time. You know, this is all I remember. These tiny little things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he talked to me. He showed me the city. And he met some Jewish people he knew from Berlin, and so on and so forth. After eight days we went to Aachen. Aachen is a city not far away from Cologne, which is on the border of Germany and Belgium. This is a-- really a border town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed at a farmhouse for another eight days. And he again talked to me and explained what is going to be. What it was going to be is that we will go illegally out of Germany, crossing the border and walking all night. This is December '38. This is snow like this on the ground. Cold. It is cold. So after eight days, finally, the farmhouse owner comes into our room and says, Mr. Posener, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. The time has arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went into another house. Went into a room. And in the corner, there were two younger men sitting there, waiting. And we said, good evening. Good evening. He closes the door. He said, goodbye. Closes the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about five minutes later, just vis-a-vis another big door opens. And two huge Gestapo men come in with a little fellow in the middle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I recognized the little fellow. It was the son of the farmhouse owner.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they greeted us very friendly. Good evening. I hope you had a nice trip so far. And I'm sure that you don't have anything to declare. And the gentleman here will be your guide. And we hope you have a fruitful trip. Good luck to you. What is going on? Why are they so friendly? They hate the Jews. Why the friendliness?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-ha! There's a trick to anything. In order to cross illegally the border, my father had to pay 250 marks for him and 250 marks for me. That was 500 marks. In those days, that was good money. Out of the 500 marks, the farmhouse owner got a cut. His son, the guide, got a cut. And the two Gestapo people got a cut. Now, you imagine the hundreds of people who went across the border. What amount of money that made. Now comes a nick in the nutshell. We started crossing the border. And by about 12:30 in the morning, suddenly there's a big yell-- gendarmes! That meant the border police. The Belgian gendarmes. They were looking for the Jews crossing into Belgium. We were running and hiding. And as I understood later, from my dad, that if they found you, they send you back. And they got a cut.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we started all over again. This was all Jewish money. Unfortunately, thousands of people tried that once, tried it twice, and three times. And they didn't have any money anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't cross the borders. Without a guide, impossible. So they were all sent back to Austria, back to the inland Germany. And that's where they were deported from later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we continued walking. All I can remember is saying every five minutes to my dad, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Are we already there? [SPEAKING GERMAN]? How long? Papa, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Dad, I'm tired. I'm cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, by 6 o'clock in the morning, we arrived at a border, and there was a limousine waiting already. It was all involved with the whole thing. And in the moment we hit the limousine, one of the young fellows took his arm around me. The car left, and I fell asleep. Sound asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: By about 10 o'clock in the morning, I woke up, and we were in Brussels. And the reason I knew it-- my dad says, we're in Brussels. See the policeman? This is a traffic cop out of Belgium, with a white helmet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first day we went into-- of course, we were wet, cold. The first day, we went to the committee. They give us some money to live on. About 90-- 80 francs, for the both of us. And I got a new pants. A long pants. No more shorts. Long pants. And we hunted for an apartment. So we found one. The two fellows slept in one bed, and my dad and I slept in one bed. [INAUDIBLE] I will never forget that. And by about the second-- ah, first week, second week of January, 1939, we found another apartment. A little tiny mansarde. The mansarde, in French, it's just a tiny little room. It's not even a four by four. I mean, it is-- if it is maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 15 feet by 15 feet, max. There was a bed in there, with an armoire where you put your clothing in. A table, a chair, and the window up to the roof so that you had some air, a potbelly stove, and the bathroom was on the second floor, so you had a little pot to go at night, if you didn't want to go in the cold down there. And every morning, I had to clean it, of course. You know. And I had to go back to school, which I hated like a sin. But I had to go back to school. That was a law, and that was it. My dad instilled in me-- even if you hate school, even if you don't like school-- the law says you go. You never play hooky, at no time. You were there at 8 o'clock sharp, and you never come late. This is all instilled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life goes on. What does a man like my dad do so? I learned French very easily. Hey, I'm having a good time. The vacation comes. I work in a grand hotel as a little page boy for one month.

speaker: interviewee
text: I make more-- twice the amount of money in tips that he made from the committee money. So my dad is happy. Then he puts me on the little cutter in Antwerp for a month to learn what the sea is like. So I'm having a good time. He, on the other hand, like all the men in this age, he's about 50 years old, 52. They don't know what to do with themselves. The shock of having to leave their family, the shock of having to leave their life, they were not prepared for that, neither physically nor emotionally. The fear they had living under this chaos stayed with them. They were this way. They didn't know what to do. My mother had an aunt in New York and an uncle in Colorado. And my dad corresponded with them every month. And they send him sometimes a $10 bill in a letter. You know, so that was good. She also had an aunt in Argentina, in Buenos Aires. Very well-to-do people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the beginning of '39-- Ann Cohen was her name-- sent 13 affidavits to Europe for her family. The only problem was that my mother was in Germany. And the affidavit to go to America was in her name. And she couldn't leave. They didn't let her out anymore. So we got stuck.

speaker: interviewer
text: She couldn't ask for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. I was supposed to go over in the aliyah in 1936, to Palestine, and I didn't want to leave. I said, I want to stay with my dad. It was-- one of my cousins left. He still lives in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1939-- in the mid '39-- the American embassy accepted a certain amount of children to go to the United States who have a relationship over there. And I refused again. I said, I want to stay with you, Dad. I don't want to go. How meshuga could I have gotten? But you don't now foresee this. So my dad goes to the coffee shops, the coffee houses. They play gin rummy. They play pen. They play all kinds of card games. The interesting parts to this, what do they say? And it's very typical to remember what I told you in 1933 when he talked to my mother. What is going to happen to me? The same thing they're saying this. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. When it's all over, we go back home. They still hadn't learned. There was no more going home. Where? To what? You became stateless in the moment you left Germany. You had no nationality. He-- Hitler-- decreed that. What do you-- they still haven't understood their pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: You keep-- you know, the German Jew was as arrogant as the Germans were. There's just no two ways about it. And he stayed this way. In the thinking, in his behavior, in everything. And like, life goes on. Nothing lasts forever. You read in the paper-- I translate to my dad from the French into the German that they're gearing up for war, and so on. And Chamberlain and all this nonsense. So by 1940, the 10th of May, I hear a noise on the roof. And my dad says, go down-- we still sleep in one bed-- and go down and get the paper. Or get some news. I go down, and somebody says, German are invading Belgium. [GASPS] I go zoom up five flights, and say, Dad. Germany invades Belgium. He says, I knew it. Go up on the roof and pick up the piece of shrapnel. It's a piece of metal. From a grenade, or whatever it was. And he was right. He's an old warhorse. By 10 o'clock, there's a rap on the door. There are two policemen. And they say, Mr. Posener, please take the necessities. You have to come with us. In every country, when a war starts, all the foreigners get to be interned. Especially the ones you are fighting with, from their country. Jews or not Jews-- that was neither here nor there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we take the necessity, whatever it is. We go to the city hall, and there are already hundreds of men waiting here. And we wait and wait. Then the lieutenant came from the police department and took everybody's name. Checked it off on the list. And he checked off my father's name. And he says, who's this? He says, this is my son. He says, well, how old is he? He just turned 14. He says, can't go. My father said, what do you mean, he can't go? He's too young to be interned. My father says, what am I going to do with him? He says, well, he's going to have to go back home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Huh! My dad says, well, where are we going? He says, we go to the La Petite Garrison, which was just about four blocks from there. It was a garrison-- military garrison. And so my dad gave me a school paper, saying that I am Louis Posener, living Rue de la Bonne, number-- number five in Brussels, and I was registered at city hall. So I go home. And he says, come back. Make a suitcase up for you and me. Come back tomorrow morning. And by this time, I will get you. OK. I get back to the little mansarde. Have nothing else to do but to look for cigarettes. Right? Start puffing the cigarettes my father left. Packing a suitcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next morning, I schlep the suitcase. The suitcase is heavier than me. This is dumb. And I'm already cussing and swearing. I'm already unhappy. Nobody on the streets. Everybody left Belgium. They were so afraid of the Germans, remembering 1914-18.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come to the garrison. And there's a French-- Belgian soldier in front. And I says, hi! He says, hi. I said, I want to see my dad. He says, who's your dad? I said, well, he was-- came in here last night. Oh! They left already at 3 o'clock in the morning. I said, to where? To Schaerbeek. I said, Schaerbeek? That's an hour and a half away from here, and there ain't no streetcars. He says, I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is the little fellow, schlepping the stupid suitcase. It took me three and a half hours to get up there. And as I arrive in the big confinement in Schaerbeek, there are thousands of men--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking!

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked three and a half--

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlepping the suitcase! Behind me, carrying it. [SCOFFS] I mean, it's horrendous. And there are thousands of men up there. And I see one man I know. I says, what's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, I have no idea. You will never find out. Forget it. We're leaving in an hour. I said, where are you going? He says, we have no idea. We're being shipped out by trains. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I sit on the suitcase. And instead of crying, or instead of feeling bad, I'm angry. I am so angry. I'm cussing and swearing. I'm kicking the stupid suitcase. I said, to hell with it. I took the suitcase. Another three hours, I went back again. Schlepped it upstairs. And there is no food. The whole house was empty-- the apartment house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- you know what a passe-partout is? That's a passkey. And I-- I still have them, the two passkeys-- would you believe me-- at home. And I went into every apartment and looked for bread. Whatever they had. So I found some old breads and here and there to eat something. And smoked the rest of the cigarettes. What else are you going to do? There's nothing else you're going to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: After about three, four days, one of my buddies comes-- Werner-- and knocks on the door. I says, Werner. You didn't go, either. He says, nope. Dad went, but not me. Mother's at home. She says to come home to-- to us. I said, I can't leave here. He Says, come on up. You'll have something to eat. You will fix something to eat. You can stay the night, and then go home again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went up. And the mother talked to us and said, the Germans will come. It may be just two days. But they will enter. They're already over the bridges. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said to Werner, what are we going to do? He says, I got a terrific idea. When they come through the train station-- because officers will come and civilians will come eventually-- we will sell them pencils and postcards. I said, are you crazy? He says, no. And underneath all the postcards, we sell dirty pictures. They will buy it. Somewhat was appealing. So about two weeks later, I stayed at home-- you know, my little mansarde-- and I had a little food here and there. And then finally, the big trucks came. And I saw the German soldiers come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let me tell you something. If anybody says anything as far as the German Army is concerned, it's a lie. They were gods. They had the world in the palm of their hands. They were six-footers. They were blond. They were powerful. They overran Europe. You couldn't stop them if you wanted to. The French Army, the Belgian Army, the Holland Army were no match for them. Aside of the espionage. These guys were gods. They were so self-conscious, so elated. You couldn't stop them, at no time. And I'm watching them going by, and just-- and I'm already up in the air again. I'm angry to see the uniforms. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. From the water into the bathtub. Didn't go any place. So about two weeks later, Werner comes. And he's got a lot of postcards and pencils, and I look at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have no idea. Never asked him. And I look at the dirty pictures. Of course, you know, hey-- 14 years old, man. That's cool. So we put them underneath, and we went to the train station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were holding up the cards, and opening like a poker game, a little tiny card where the dirty picture was. We sold them like hotcake. Are you kidding? It was two francs for it. It was always two postcards and a dirty picture and a pencil. Two francs. We made money-- I mean, hand over fist. That was terrific. We did it for about four days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, once the Army was in and took over, civilians came. So they needed Cognac. They wanted butter. They wanted nylons. I mean, the-- the silk stockings. They didn't have any nylons yet. They wanted materials for suits. And you had already ration cards. We went into the black market. I mean, he was a year and a half older than I was. And if we bought materials-- which you need three yards for a suit with a vest. If we bought it for 100 francs, we sold it to them for 5,000 francs. I have never seen that much money in my lifetime. I mean, the young kid goes into the bars. You can smoke cigarettes when you're 14 over there. You can get alcohol. We were sitting, playing poker, losing everything, winning everything. It was a heyday. Stupid. I was so dumb. It could have killed me. I didn't pay any attention. I should've known better. Nothing lasts forever. I'm having a good time. And then, by-- by the end of '40-- '40-- 1940 till about mid '41, the black market petered out somehow because otherwise, guys got wise. I worked in the-- in the nightclub from 8 o'clock in the evening till 5 o'clock in the morning. There were two young fellows who had it open. And I saw the nude dancers and all this. And I had a good time just cleaning the tables and all that. But again, nothing lasts forever. But again and again, I was so stupid not to pay attention. Because at this stage of the game, I already knew what was going on. I was taught. I saw. I learned. I still didn't pay any attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: By January 1942, I walk on the streets. And I don't know where I went. Somebody said hello to me. I says, bonjour. Hello. And he says, I've been observing you. I says, have you really? Cocky, like a streetwise kid. I'm-- you know, I lived with pimps, with whores, with everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent time with everything! And-- matter of fact, I think my first woman was 38 years old, and I was 14 and a half. That kind of a rowdy kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, you're doing pretty well for yourself. How would you like to work for me? Famous words. Doing what and how much?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, there's no money involved. We need little guys like you, who are cocky, who are strong. We need them for couriers. A courier is a runner. One of the famous runners was Audrey Hepburn, for Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what am I supposed to do? He says, well, I'll show you the places where to go. And you carry letters from one spot to the other. OK. I do this about-- Did he tell you who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No names.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you know who you would be working for, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was somebody already waiting whenever I went to that spot. And just handed me something. Put it in my pocket. Went to another spot. There was somebody waiting there. Picked it up, and that was it. No names. Basically, I found out it was the Belgian resistance. That's all I found out. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew that from the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that from the beginning. Because he also mentioned, we work for the Belgian resistance. If you don't want to, you don't have to. I said, no! Hey! That's adventure! Little guy was-- adrenaline going. And unfortunately, it didn't last too long. I was so visible. Every Tom, Dick, and Harry knew me. I was so blond. The SS-- the Flemish SS knew me from bars, from coffee houses and all that. In June of '42, I'm walking home, and I'm in the North Station. It's called the Gare du Nord. And two Flemish SS men come toward me. And I said to myself, uh-oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one says, hi, little fella! I says, hi. Aren't you Louis? I mean, that wasn't my real name, but I'm using this name. He says, no? Oh, come on, now. Aren't you Jew? I says, no. We know your name is Posener. I said, no. And this went on for about two, three minutes. And then suddenly, I have a big fist on my nose. And I'm starting to dribble blood out of my nose. I said, oh, Jesus. They will find out anyway. Why-- why kill me? So I agreed to whatever they said.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me to what they call a hofbrauhaus-- one of their houses where all the military is-- into the basement. And I got a little solo box match, where they pummeled me up. And they-- as a little guy. You know, I'm about five foot two.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this age?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 16.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just 16 two months. And they pummel me up good. And then they take me to the streetcar. And I'm just a little hootenanny. I'm about five foot two. I may weigh about 130, max. And they put me in the corner of the streetcars and have two Mauser guns in my ribs. Like I'm going to go someplace. The people in the streetcars were laughing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They take me to Gestapo headquarter on Avenue Louise. And there I met Lieutenant Frank. And all he does, first of all-- he picks me up by my jacket and lifts me up on a wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: I turned pale. I could feel it. I peed in my pants. Definitely. It was soaking. And he says, [TSKS] you Jews are something, aren't you? Goes to his desk. Lets me down again. Goes to his desk. Picks up my papers. He says, Louis Posener, Berlin, [INAUDIBLE] the whole shaboom. He's got it all in front of him. What am I going to tell him? He says, but you know something? You look pretty strong. We're going to put you in a labor camp. The Belgian authorities will register you someplace. It'll be good for you. You can work for the German government.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea what he's talking about. I'd been put in a basement of the-- the Gestapo. I stayed there two weeks, while other people come in. Finally, the day comes-- this is like, the 24th. 24Th of June, 1942. Early in the morning, we all assemble at the train station. Other people come from other places. We're about 45 men out of Belgium. Most of them-- matter of fact, all of them-- came from Antwerp. There were Polish Jews who came to Belgium in 1902, 1924, and those years, who were, you know, 60 year old, 55 year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were all put together with guards-- German military guards-- to go into the train. OK? And as we march into the train-- Let's wait right now, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --We need to change the tape again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.
